response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm not on social media that much, maybe a few times a month when I'm bored or someone tells me to check something out, so I don't get caught up in all the drama about fake news and misinformation like some people do. But honestly, when I do scroll through, it's pretty obvious that a lot of what you see is just garbage. Working retail in DC, I talk to people all day long, and you'd be surprised how many folks believe the wildest things they saw on Facebook or wherever. It's actually kind of scary sometimes. My approach is pretty straightforward and I think most people could benefit from it. First off, if something sounds too crazy or too perfect, it probably is. I'm not the most organized person and I don't spend hours fact-checking everything, but common sense goes a long way. Like when I see some post claiming politicians are doing something absolutely insane, I just think about whether that even makes sense in the real world. Usually it doesn't. I also tend to trust what I hear from people I actually know and talk to face-to-face more than random posts online. When I'm out rock climbing or hiking with friends, we talk about real stuff, not whatever viral nonsense is going around that week. That keeps me grounded. The thing is, I'm pretty social and I like engaging with people, but I've learned not to get into arguments online about what's real or fake. It's just not worth the energy. If someone shares something sketchy, I might mention it to them in person next time I see them, but I'm not going to start a comment war. My parents are both educated people and they always taught me to question things, even if I didn't finish much formal schooling myself. So my advice is simple: don't believe everything you see, talk to real people, use your brain, and don't spend so much time online that you lose perspective. There's a whole world out there beyond the screen, and that's where the truth usually lives anyway.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 34-year-old immigrant retail worker in DC with lower secondary education but educated parents, who spends minimal time on social media (few times monthly). High extraversion suggests social, talkative approach with face-to-face preference. Low openness means practical, traditional thinking rather than complex analytical approaches. Low conscientiousness suggests casual, less systematic methods. The moderate agreeableness and neuroticism create balanced, reasonable tone without extreme reactions. Hobbies like hiking and rock climbing suggest outdoor, real-world focus. Atheist background may contribute to skeptical, evidence-based thinking. The response should reflect practical wisdom from customer service experience, preference for in-person interaction, common-sense approach over technical solutions, and casual engagement with social media issues.
response_parsed.tone: Conversational, practical, straightforward, socially-oriented but not overly analytical, grounded in real-world experience
